draft.introduction: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789) established the foundational principles of the French Revolution: natural rights, popular sovereignty, and the rule of law. It remains a cornerstone of international human rights law.

translation.original_ref: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789)
translation.text: Men are born and remain free and equal in rights. Social distinctions may be based only on common utility. The aim of every political association is the preservation of the natural and imprescriptible rights of man: liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression. The principle of sovereignty resides essentially in the nation. No body and no individual may exercise authority that does not expressly emanate from it.
translation.original_text: Les hommes naissent et demeurent libres et égaux en droits. Les distinctions sociales ne peuvent être fondées que sur l’utilité commune.

translator_notes.note: The declaration’s assertion that men are “born free and equal” draws on Rousseau, while the list of natural rights (liberty, property, security, resistance to oppression) modifies Locke. Lafayette drafted the initial text with advice from Jefferson, making it a transatlantic synthesis of Enlightenment thought.
